narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands well above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 42, higher than the average of 36.70—these reflect your dedication and potential in a field where perseverance is key. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 is notably lower than the average of 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 3 falls below the typical 5.53, suggesting some early academic hurdles in your schooling journey. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, which is below the dataset average, and you're planning to attend full-time, a common choice among most students. The admissions model, trained on past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 48% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—this threshold led to the tough decision to reject your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed on that critical exam. The most influential factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your lower Year 1 decile, which signaled early struggles compared to peers who ranked higher. On the positive side, your standout LSAT score strongly pushed the model toward expecting you to pass, acting as your biggest asset. Your strong undergraduate GPA also helped, nudging the prediction in a favorable direction relative to typical applicants. Meanwhile, your family's income level contributed somewhat toward the failure risk, as lower resources can impact preparation in ways the model has observed historically, though your Year 3 decile had a milder negative pull. Overall, while your test scores shine brighter than most, the early decile rankings tipped the balance against you in this data-driven review. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking scholarships or support tailored for Black women in law to address income-related gaps, and reapply with an even stronger profile—your LSAT and GPA already set you apart, so building on that foundation could turn this around. We're rooting for your success in law.
